paragraphs.text: Phase 1 – Pre bid support to December [20], 2024
A. FINANCIAL DUE DILIGENCE

Unless otherwise noted, our work will concentrate on historical financial information of the latest fiscal years ended December 31,2022 (“FY22”), 2023 (“FY23”) and the latest trailing twelve months period (e.g. TTM Aug-24).
Our analysis in this phase will be limited to the sell-side financial due diligence materials.
Sell-side Quality of Earnings assessment

Based on the analysis and discussion with the sell-side due diligence advisor, comment on Management’s proposed adjustments to reported EBITDA (i.e. Executive compensation changes, personal expenses, one-time / non-recurring items, out-of-period adjustments, truck recovery rate increases, other etc.) and inquire about the nature and rationale of such adjustments and supporting documentation;
Perform a traffic light assessment of Management and sell-side proposed adjustments;
Propose any significant adjustments identified that are not reflected in the sell-side Quality of Earnings analysis; and
Comment on any potential findings that could have material impacts to enterprise / equity value.
Phase 2 – Post bid support beyond December [20], 2024
B. FINANCIAL DUE DILIGENCE
Overview
Obtain background information of Target, including its:
Organizational structure, ownership and management reporting relationships;
Product / service offerings;
Range of customer segments and regions served; and
Financial reporting and information systems (including process, timing, structure of financial reporting, and interaction with the information/accounting systems).
Consistency of accounting policies
Read Target’s financial statements and discuss them with Target’s management to gain an understanding of accounting policies and practices including:
Reporting methodology;
Intercompany transactions and cost allocations;
Revenue recognition policies and billing practices;
Significant accounting estimates; and
Recent or contemplated changes in accounting principles.

Quality of earnings – To the extent not covered by the sell-side due diligence report. Assessment of any subsequent roll-forward of the sell-side quality of earnings report (if available) and propose potential incremental adjustments identified by considering:
Management’s proposed adjustments to reported EBITDA;
The impact of revenue accounting and accounting / cash timing differences including:
Understand application of revenue recognition policies, specifically POC, including consistency of application and period end true-ups that may skew financial results; and
Understand any timing differences between revenue recognition, cash collections and accrual adjustments.
The impact of any changes to senior management or team structure;
The impact of any changes to pricing models;
The impact of any changes in labour costs;
The impact of commodity prices, pricing lags and pass-through mechanisms;
The impact of foreign exchange (if any);
The pro forma impact of new Barrie Branch;
The impact of any key supplier cost arrangements;
The impact of settlements related to litigation, if any; and
The impact of cash to accrual accounting and other potential items discovered in the due diligence process.
Supporting analyses to quality/sustainability of earnings

Revenue and gross margin analysis

1. Obtain and read an analysis of Target’s revenue, margin and KPI’s and inquire about:
Revenue and margin by business segment (Services & Projects: HVAC, Compressed air, Plumbing, Chiller, Electrical, CM4, CM+, repair etc.);
Revenue and margin by customer end market (Industrial, commerical, retirement homes, schools, condos etc.);
Revenue and margin by geography (i.e. GTA, Cambridge, London, Barrie etc.);
Price vs. volume trends;
Understand revenue lifecycle of significant customers;
Any non-recurring components of revenue;
Effectiveness of cross-selling programs;
Revenue seasonality;
Backlog and pipeline/committed revenue; and
Other KPIs as made available by Target (i.e. length of service contract, renewal / retention rates, staff utilization, volumes etc)
2. Summarize value creation recommendations based on the above analysis.
Expenses
Obtain and read an analysis of Target’s operating expenses and inquire about:
Employee compensation and benefits:
Headcount and average compensation costs;
Turnover and average tenure of employees at key locations over time;
Accrued employee obligations and other provisions;
Severance / commitments, if any; and
Incentive based compensation.
Fixed vs. variable expense analysis;
Effectiveness of sales and marketing programs;
Insurance; and
Other operating and SG&A expenses.
FY24 Outturn

Obtain the FY24 budget and forecast for the rest of the year. Interview management about the reasons for significant fluctuations with budget. Discuss and bridge to full-year FY24 forecast with management. Comment on predictability of current year’s performance and level of confidence in achieving the 2024 budget.
Working capital
Summarize and normalize historical working capital in the business including:
Commenting on the working capital trends, for the respective period;
Understanding key drivers (i.e., seasonality, accounts payable days, days sales outstanding, inventory days); and
Identifying large or unusual non-recurring items that may have impacted normal working capital trends including potential items identified within the quality of earnings analysis noted above.
Supporting analyses to working capital
Accounts receivable
Obtain and read an analysis of Target’s accounts receivable and inquire about:
Aging analysis and related credit terms (including special terms);
Trade and non-trade balances;
Allowance for uncollectible accounts and write-offs; and
Reserve and adjustments.
Prepaid expenses
Obtain a summary of and comment on unusual items, significant fluctuations, and significant balances.
Accounts payable, accrued liabilities and other current liabilities
Obtain and read an analysis of Target’s accounts payable, accrued liabilities and other current liabilities and inquire about:
AP aging analysis and days outstanding;
Terms of trade with major vendors;
Revenue arrangements and timing of payments;
Payroll related funding and timing of cash flows;
Cadence and consistency of bonus/commission and other incentive compensation; and
Other current and non-current liabilities.
Net debt
Summarize and comment on net debt items (on and off balance sheet) presented by management and other potential debt-like items; and
Consider whether elements of working capital have the nature of and may be reclassified as net debt, and if so, summarize the potential impact of these adjustments on working capital and net debt.
Capital expenditure
Obtain and read an analysis of Target’s existing capital costs and inquire about:
Maintenance versus growth capital expenditures;
Other historical, deferred, and planned capital expenditures; and
Impairment write-downs and issues.
Related parties
Understand the nature and extent of related party transactions and basis for pricing arrangements.
Commitments and contingencies
Inquire about significant commitments and contingent liabilities including:
Pending or threatened litigations by regulatory or other authorities and by employees;
Incentive compensation and employee benefit obligations;
Significant lease and purchase obligations; and
Inquire about other off-balance sheet transactions.
Optional FDD procedures
Assistance with transaction documents (optional)
Review purchase agreement and all relevant definitions (e.g. NWC, debt-like items definitions and adjustment procedures).

Calls with R&W insurers / lenders
Conduct call with potential R&W insurers / lenders and answer any Q&A as it pertains to our engagement.
Discussion with external accountant and review of work papers (to extent available)
Obtain and read year-end review working papers for the last fiscal year and perform the following:
Discuss any significant issues with the external accountants;
Inquire of the engagement partner about the existence of any disagreements with management;
Comment on the nature and volume of year-end accounting differences (adjustments), use of accounting estimates, any changes in accounting policies or methods and their implications; and
Read management reports prepared by the external accountants and comment on control issues.
